```suggestion
By default, Ray tasks require 1 CPU each, so the scheduler limits task concurrrency to the available CPUs.
Tasks that use more than 1 CPU (e.g., via mutlithreading) may experience slowdown due to interference from concurrent tasks, but otherwise are safe to run.

However, tasks that use more than their proportionate share of memory may overload a node and cause issues like OOM.
If that is the case, we can reduce the number of concurrently running tasks by increasing the amount of resources requested by those tasks.
This works because Ray makes sure that the sum of the resource requirements of all of the concurrently running tasks on a given node does not exceed the node's total resources.
```

A couple of scars I have from using `s.replace`:

- The second argument is a regex, so you need to escape a bunch of special characters with `\`, which means the string should be "raw" (see https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html).
- I generally prefer to use triple-quoted strings for any multi-line replacements:  it is easier to read.

So, for your example, it would be:

```python
s.replace(
    "google/cloud/python-storage/noxfile.py",
    r"UNIT_TEST_PYTHON_VERSIONS = \["2\.7", "3\.6", "3\.7", "3\.8", "3\.9"\]",
    """\
UNIT_TEST_PYTHON_VERSIONS = ["2.7", "3.6", "3.7", "3.8", "3.9"]
CONFORMANCE_TEST_PYTHON_VERSIONS = ["3.8"]"""
)
```

In my project I'm using:

```
    :hide_key: "/**/examples","/**/_comment"
```

To remove all `examples` and `_comment` fields anywhere in the JSON document.
I'm also using:

```
    :hide_key_if_empty: "/**/default"
```

To hide `default` fields anywhere in the document if the default value is empty.

I could, for example do:
```
    :hide_key: "/$defs/**/_comment"
```
To remove `_comment` fields from anything that descends from `$defs`. 
Or:
```
    :hide_key: "/$defs/*/description"
```

To hide the `description` field of anything directly defined in `$defs`.

```
git checkout master
```

```
git commit
```

> Regarding the global COUNTERS variable... I'm not sure that's the best approach.
It could be read/updated not only by web requests, but by cronjobs also or even by one-off Dyno commands.

This is what the `COUNTERS_LOCK` is for:  It ensures that each thread in a process has exclusive accesses the COUNTERS.

> The multi thread lock won't suffice for the cross-process problem.

That is what `transaction.atomic():` is for: Ensuring the `db_counter` is unique among all the processes that access the database.

> On SO, people advice against using global shared variables, as Django has a "shared-nothing" architecture.

I completely agree with the philosophy of the "shared nothing" architecture, unfortunately Django and Treeherder are not using that philosophy:  The `AUTO INCREMENT` columns are shared global variables.  `AUTO INCREMENT` columns should not be used:  They are convenient for small apps, but do not scale.

This PR is an attempt to avoid a large database migration, and replace the `AUTO INCREMENT` feature which provides a dense, non-overlapping, id numbers that fit in `INT32`.  Since `AUTO INCREMENT` is a global variable, it makes sense the replacement will act similar.. 

This code has two levels:  One, to serve chunks of ids to the various machines/processes, and another to coordinate the serving of individual ids to each of the threads.

We pass in a SQL() object in the test. In the DBM logic we generate another SQL() object containing the dbm comment and we add those two SQL objects together. Adding two SQL() objects produces the composed object in the assertion. According to the psycopg spec creating a composed object from two SQL object is the preferred way of modifying a query: https://www.psycopg.org/docs/sql.html#sql-objects.

ex: `SQL("/* dbm comment */") + SQL("/* select *; */") -> Composed([SQL("/* dbm comment */"), SQL("/* select *; */"])`

Both SQL() and Composed() implement the Composable interface. But you're right they're not the same type.
`isinstance(psycopg.sql.SQL('h')), psycopg.sql.Composable)== True`
`isinstance(psycopg.sql.Composed([psycopg.sql.SQL('h'), psycopg.sql.SQL('h')]), psycopg.sql.Composable)== True`
`isinstance(psycopg.sql.SQL('h'), psycopg.sql.Composed)== False`

To answer your questions:
1. Yes,  this logic converts SQL() objects to Composed(). But if a composed object is passed to `cursor.execute()` then we don't change the type we simply append to it. 
2. This behavior is expected and shouldn't cause any unexpected side effects.

Alternative approach:

-  SQL() is passed to cursor.execute() get the raw string and create a new SQL() object with the DBM object
- If Composed() is passed to cursor.execute() create a SQL() object with the DBM comment and append it to the existing Composed. 

The con with this alternative approach is that it goes against psycopg composable conventions.


@brettlangdon  do you have any strong feelings? We can move forward with the alternative approach and put this PR on hold for now.

I think this is the difference between a "natural" spline (what `setupSpline` does) and a "Catmull-Rom" spline.  The natural spline is smoother, since it enforces the continuous first derivatives.  To get that smoothness though, you have to solve ~N simultaneous (though  sparse) equations.  This isn't so bad in 1D - it's inverting a tri-diagonal matrix.  In 2+D though, you lose the tridiagonality since points in more than one dimension aren't well-ordered.  It's still sparse though, so I'm sure it's still possible, but I didn't try it.  (...and all the bicubic spline algorithms I found online were either the Catmull-Rom type, or the cubic convolution, which makes a third variant).

So it is a different design and will yield different results than what we do in 1D.  I think that's okay though; I don't think I'd advocate changing either one.

FWIW, one potential advantage of the Catmull-Rom spline is that it's local.  Changing the value of a point only changes the interpolation near that point, which isn't true for the natural spline.

> This sounds much more like a use case for allowing user transactions to contain clarity code to me, rather than including predicates in the wire format-- whether or not you own a name after executing a function is pretty straight-forward to test in clarity. So, if the plan is to support transactions with raw clarity code, then we probably shouldn't support any predicates apart from on transfers.

This appears to contradict my model of your understanding of how transactions work.  Right now, I think that you think that there are only two transaction types -- one for defining a smart contract (which has the side-effect of also instantiating a smart contract account), and one for calling a method in an existing smart contract (and does not instantiate a new smart contract account).  Are you saying that we need to expand the second transaction type to be able to execute any Clarity code?

I'm strongly of the opinion that our transaction wire format should offer first-class support for post-conditions on the transaction's originating account _outside of_ the Clarity code.  In my SIP 005 draft, the rationale is:

```
Logically speaking, the post-conditions in a transaction are _representable_ as a single
Clarity statement that is evaluated immediately after the successful evaluation of the
transaction code body.  The post-conditions would be representable as a single
conjunction of a sequence of boolean statements containing only asset queries,
comparators, and literals.  This conjunction would be nested within an `(if
...)` statement that would evaluate an `(err ...)` if the conjunction was false
(i.e. at least one post-condition was false), thereby causing the transaction to
abort.

However, because we consider post-conditions to be such a vital safety feature,
post-conditions are _encoded_ in the transaction _outside of_ the Clarity code
body.  In particular, they are encoded in a compressed format that losslessly
decompresses to the above Clarity codes snippit.  Thus, post-conditions do not
pose a violation of the Stacks design principle of showing all code "as-is" in the
blockchain wire format.

The reasons for encoding post-conditions this way are three-fold.  First, by
treating post-conditions as a first-class transaction component, the protocol guarantees
that they will be applied regardless of the contained Clarity code.  While the
Clarity code can have its own statements that implement _de-facto_
post-conditions, the act of placing post-conditions on the originating account's
assets outside the code body guarantees that they will always be run.
Second, by treating them as a separate field, we make it much easier for wallet 
developers to encode them -- namely, the wallet does not need to consider 
or even access the Clarity code body to process post-conditions.  Third, 
encoding post-conditions separately from the code body allows us to make
them cheaper to store and evaluate than had they been defined as Clarity code.
```

> I'm also not convinced we wouldn't want predicates on principals other than the sender -- a user that is transacting through a smart contract wallet may wish to only spend so much out of that contract principal.

100% agreed that there will be cases where we will want this.  However, I think that post-conditions on a transaction's originating account (which is usually also the spending account) will be _so common_ and offer such a huge security win that it's worth it to make them as cheap as possible to evaluate and as simple as possible to encode.  In particular:

* It should be as simple as possible for a wallet to transform the post-conditions into a GUI of check-boxes and form fields and back
* It should be possible for a block explorer to represent the post conditions as a terminating snippit of Clarity code in the transaction
* It should be as cheap as possible to evaluate post-conditions (they can't be free) in order to encourage wide-spread use.

1. We have a plunger laying on its side angled away from us so that half of the plunger is within the visibility distance and half is beyond the visibility distance.
2. The part of the plunger that is within the visibility distance is occluded while the portion of the plunger that is outside our visibility distance is not.

```
before pandas transposing (consistent frame):
   a b |   c d

after pandas transposing (inconsistent frame, because of different indices at the same row):
```

Hi @dismirlian 

802.15.4 defines its macAckWaitDuration from the end of the transmitted packet to complete reception of the ACK. RAIL's ackTimeout only covers sync word detection of the ACK. Therefore,  we have to subtract the ACK's PHY header and payload time to get RAIL's ackTimeout setting. For 2.4 GHz OQPSK, macAckWaitDuration is specified as 54 symbols; subtracting 2-symbol PHY header and 10-symbol payload yields a RAIL ackTimeout of 42 symbols or 672 microseconds at 16 microseconds/symbol.

This has also been documented in the Silabs RAIL document here:
https://docs.silabs.com/rail/latest/group-i-e-e-e802-15-4

This kind of thing makes me worry about the whole project of splitting everything into separate repos. It's one of the pathologies of software development teams. Teams don't like the problems with their monorepo, and so they want to split it all up. But splitting it all up creates a mess of dependencies that wants to be managed.
- We have no way of running tests across repos. If I change something in the root `allennlp` library, I want the automated tests for my dependencies to run. Google has a system for this. We don't. We already struggled with this in Scala for S2, and never solved the problem.
- We can't guarantee that one version of `allennlp-reading-comprehension` always runs with a corresponding version of `allennlp`. So now we have to write backwards-compatible code every step of the way, or think hard about version pins for our own code. In practice, many projects will require work in multiple repos, so I now have a compatibility problem within a single project, and I have to carefully manage what gets merged when. This is why you had to have `allennlp-reading-comprehension` depend on a SHA. This is why the tests in that repo don't run right now.
- Circular dependencies, like we have in this case, always mean you want to split things up even more, making the problem worse. When you work on Hotflip2, do you want to coordinate changes in `allennlp`, `allennlp-reading-comprehension`, and `allennlp-interpret`?
- Back to tests: With lots of little repos, the ideal test is isolated to running in just one of them. It is hard to make a cross-repo test. So where do we stick integration tests? In my experience, unit tests are 90% useless, but integration tests are the ones that find bugs.

I wonder if we should generate some UUID constant at the top of the file. Then to take the lock we set an annotation on the `DaskWorkerGroup` like `kubernetes.dask.org/scale-in-progress` and set the value to that UUID.

Then as part of trying to take the lock we check for that annotation. If it exists and the UUID matches we know the current controller is busy scaling in another coroutine, so we wait. If the UUID doesn't match we can assume the controller was restarted or failed over and the in-progress scale has been aborted. So we can update the annotation with our UUID and take the lock.

Then at the end, we delete the annotation again to release the lock.

In general as a policy I think we should try to avoid removing existing public APIs unless we have a good reason/justification. This helps keep the APIs backward compatible. Same with changing existing APIs (e.g., adding new parameters, etc). We can always add new public APIs.

In the case here:
- I am not sure if we have good justification for removing the `Enable/Disable()` APIs.
     - We may argue that these APIs are not widely used and may be fine to change now, but I think it is better to not set a precedence.
- We can add `SetEnabled()` along with `Enable/Disable()` but then 
    -  It can be confusing (why we have multiple APIs doing similar thing and what is the difference) and 
    - I am not sure if it does help simplify the code much (we move an `if` check from caller side to the API side).

> Could you please elaborate about this line?
> > $module = "$env:ChocolateyInstall\helpers\chocolateyProfile.psm1"

After installing OpenCL you need to refresh the environment variables in the shell in order for programs to find it; chocolatey (which is pre-installed in the vs2017-win2016 image) has a RefreshEnv command to do this, but it wasn't working properly, even though it said it was succeeding.  

It seems that the way PS is invoked in the pipeline, it runs without a startup profile script which would normally contain lines like those above.  Without them, the RefreshEnv command was aliased incorrectly so that it was updating the environment variables in the outer shell "cmd.exe", not the current powershell session that we're running the tests in.  Including these lines in the test script just resets the command alias in the PS session so that RefreshEnv updates the correct environment and the LightGBM library finds the OpenCL runtime.

Since others will probably have the same question you did, I'll add a short comment in the script.

> could you please estimate how far we are from changing default device type to gpu in Windows wheels?

There were issues related to getting the pipeline to run at all and other issues related to actual test results (mostly floating point tolerance issues, but a few crashes iirc).  I believe we have solved the former in this PR, I haven't had a chance to investigate the latter yet, so I'll need a bit more time give an estimate and work up a PR if necessary on that. 

> I remember you used OpenCL Runtime from Intel. Are there any problems with it that we should propagate to LightGBM users in our docs?

I've run against the python package using three different versions the Intel CPU OpenCL platform.  They all run successfully and give reasonable results, but for some reason they either sporadically hang at shutdown or terminate with the user script returning 'False'.  Either of these will cause the pytests to "fail".  I haven't had time track down the reason for this behavior (it might be failure in the runtime to terminate a thread at shutdown or it might be something odd about how LightGBM is using OpenCL, though that seems less likely).  So while there's definitely some issue, I'm not sure how to report this usefully in the documents.  (BTW, the same library build linked into the LightGBM cli tool works without any apparent issues using the latest Intel CPU OpenCL runtime -- the issue appears to be limited to the python wheel.)

But as part of getting to using device=gpu as the build/test default, I may be able to get a clearer picture of what's going on and of course I'll let you all know.

OA4MP is the acronym for "OAuth For MyProxy", which is the mainstream style of activation for v4 endpoints.
It requires that you open up the webapp and do an OAuth 1.0 flow with the endpoint's listed oauth server.

Not because it's exactly what you asked, but I think the full suite of v4 activation styles we see is:
- delegate-proxy (create a proxy X.509 cred by signing a user cert; here be dragons)
- oa4mp / "oauth" (the above, generating a user cert on the server which is handed back to our services)
- myproxy (send username+password to Transfer, have it exchange those for an X.509 user cert directly)
- autoactivation (almost a no-op, the X.509 user cert is owned by and created by Transfer)

This check (for a slash on the end of the path) is only required for S3 due to the way that the S3 API works. Here, you can just use `path`. Also, you should respect the `recursive` and `exist_ok` flags.

The body of this function should be something like:
```
if not recursive and not self.isdir(self.path_basename(path)):
    raise IOError("No parent directory found for {}.".format(path))
if not exist_ok and self.exists(path):
    raise IOError("Path already exists at {}.".format(path))
self.__webhdfs.make_dir(path)
```

Note: The webhdfs API `make_dir` method is equivalent to `mkdir -p` where pre-existence is not considered to be an issue.

I went back and double-checked https://github.com/openzipkin/b3-propagation and the "d" thing is only for single-header AFAICT.

For multi-header, it says only (no mention of "d" for Sampling State nor Debug Flag):
```
Sampling State

An accept sampling decision is encoded as X-B3-Sampled: 1 and a deny as X-B3-Sampled: 0. Absent means defer the decision to the receiver of this header. For example, a Sampled header might look like: X-B3-Sampled: 1.

Note: Before this specification was written, some tracers propagated X-B3-Sampled as true or false as opposed to 1 or 0. While you shouldn't encode X-B3-Sampled as true or false, a lenient implementation may accept them.
Debug Flag

Debug is encoded as X-B3-Flags: 1. Debug implies an accept decision, so don't also send the X-B3-Sampled header.
```

I agree that decoupling logic from the underlying data storage is a good goal -- I just want to make sure we're considering the implications. It sounds like removing the special postgres handling won't affect how we are doing things these days, but let me fill in a bit more historic context.

In late 2018 when I started to use Jason's original bulkdata tasks to load LDV test data for testing DSO, performance was an issue. This was true for both phases of the process: generating data in the local db, and loading it from there into Salesforce. When I profiled the latter in particular, I found that uploading the batches was quick, processing the batches was out of our control (aside from switching to parallel batches) and downloading the results was quick, but loading the result ids into the local db was slow. One change I made as a result was to put the ids in a separate table -- this allowed us to do an insert instead of update, which helped a lot. I also did quite a bit of experimentation to figure out what the fastest way was to get the ids inserted, and that's what led to using postgres rather than sqlite and using postgres' COPY FROM feature rather than sqlalchemy to load the ids.

What I'm trying to say is that I'll sign off on this refactoring even if we're losing the special postgres support, but:
1. I want to make sure Paul's aware that there's probably better performance available for loading result ids with an approach like this.
2. I would like to see a benchmark comparison for loading a significant-sized dataset before and after these changes, to make sure we're not introducing significant regressions that are unrelated to the special postgres support.

@msmttchr Indeed, this is essentially a property that evaluates to `True` when voltage protection has triggered. (And in that case the voltage should have been shut off by the instrument.) And indeed, this—and the analogous current limit property—are not settable by the user. 

Unfortunately, for `source_current_limit_enabled`, the explanation is a little more complex because when it evaluates to `True` this could mean that the source is actually being current-limited or the source has been disabled (the current protection has _tripped_, in Keithley's terminology). Given this, the name of the property feels less than ideal. In the SCPI portion of the manual, the full command is `SOURCE:CURRENT:LIMIT:STATE?` where `1` means that the output is in limit mode or has tripped, depending on what mode the user put the device in.

So, I agree, these properties names and/or doc strings could be improved. I looked around for other PSU instruments in PyMeasure and didn't find anything like this being exposed, so perhaps this is a chance to set a good precedent.

Does the use of `_enabled` in a property name imply something that can be set (with some other naming being used for properties that are read-only)? (I had only chosen `_enabled` to go along with the fact that the property is Boolean-valued, no more rationale than that.)

In any case, it seems easy to expand upon the doc string to further clarify the meanings of each of these properties, perhaps something along these lines: 

Voltage:

"A boolean property that returns the source voltage protection state. If this property is True, the source has been shut off in accordance with the source voltage protection settings. If this property is False, the source has not been shut off due to voltage protection."

Current:

"A boolean property that returns the source current limit state. If this property is True, the source is in either in current limit mode, or has tripped (shut off), based on the source_current_limit_type setting. If this property is False, the source is not being limited and has not been tripped."

``` python
def config_change(config):
    # Give users the tools but let them decide how to change the config

ex.add_config(default_config)
ex.automain(my_main_function)
```

`extractall` is unsafe because it can unzip _outside_ of the output root: https://docs.python.org/2/library/zipfile.html#zipfile.ZipFile.extractall - we should probably not use it.

Edge-case-wise, `extract` does some normalisation around leading and trailing slashes, drive names, and character-replacement (https://docs.python.org/2/library/zipfile.html#zipfile.ZipFile.extract) which it would be nice to avoid getting bitten by or needing to re-implement :)

```
  -j [N], --jobs[=N]          Allow N jobs at once; infinite jobs with no arg.

  -j N     run N jobs in parallel (0 means infinity) [default=10 on this system]
```

1. My machine is melting down -> decrease parallelism.
2. This thing is slow -> increase.

> do you guys purposefully avoid them to promote self documenting code?

No, but that's the best excuse for our laziness that I've heard yet :D That said -- yes, I would argue that docstrings are great but the more you can write code which doesn't need documentation, the better. One big problem with docstrings (and any docs) is how easy it is for them to fall out-of-sync with code; especially in situations like this where all the arguments are convention through the whole repo, having every function have the same docstring re: those copied values could quickly get out of hand!

Note that the above isn't meant to be an argument against writing docs/comments/docstrings, more of a warning of things to watch out for!

```
class _Unset: pass
```

```
self._request_id = kwargs.pop('request_id', _Unset)
```

``` 
         # user specifying None explicitly takes precedence (send nothing)
         if self._request_id is None:
            request_id = None
         
         # context options take next highest precendence
         elif 'request_id' in request.context.options:
             request_id = request.context.options.pop('request_id')

         # explicit user-specified next
         elif self._request_id is not _Unset:
             request_id = self._request_id
         
         # otherwise, auto-generate (if asked)
         elif self._auto_request_id:
             request_id = str(uuid.uuid1())

         $ finally, send nothing then
         else: 
            request_id = None

         if request_id is not None:
             header = {"x-ms-client-request-id": request_id}
             request.http_request.headers.update(header)

```

> If we can't easily reproduce the race condition, maybe we could just verify that replacing the file works by reading the Apache source code and/or just trying it and see if Apache properly uses the new response obtained by Certbot?

Apache happily and properly uses the new OCSP staples fetched and saved by Certbot if the format is correct.

Here are some error cases and how Apache handles them:

- DBM file format gets corrupted (needs magic header & file size needs to be multiple of the pagesize) 
  - Apache will print error to `ErrorLog` every time it tries to access the database, does not recreate it and is forced to re-fetch OCSP responses whenever the memory cache expires. This is the worst case scenario. Apache will cache the response in memory for some time, so this doesn't happen for each request.
- DBM key (the SHA1 hash of certificate) gets corrupted
  - Basically the same as not being able to find pre-existing certificate from DB - a new key will be created.
- DBM key value (the TTL timestamp + OCSP response) gets corrupted
  - Apache will delete the DBM and re-create it.

I actually don't think it needs to (or should) be blocking in all circumstances. Because the method can get invoked in separate threads, there's not really a benefit to one thread waiting to acquire the lock when another is already active trying to drain the buffer. Whoever gets the lock first can complete the sync and re-initialize the buffer. The only reason we want it to block when waiting to acquire the lock during shutdown is to avoid a race where the lock isn't released quickly enough by other threads during shutdown.

I'm mostly thinking that having a set is defensive for how the upstream standardisation happens. If when we talk to the other remote execution folks, consensus is to go for a dict, it's easy for us to go from set to dict (we could continue to expose a set to rules, or accept both and have a default fallback for sets, or similar). If we do a dict here, and end up specifying a set for the RE stuff, it's a breaking change for us to take away that flexibility we offered rule-writers, and I don't see particular value in the flexibility. If there's a reason for allowing the flexibility, that's a different story :)

A set also slightly avoids needing to spec a few edge cases (e.g. banning one specified cache being a subdir of another), but again, if there's a reason for the flexibility, that's fine.

> I would expect that the patience process would keep track of the best scoring model we've seen so far, and if we've not seen anything better after a certain number of partial fit calls, would stop training that model, keeping a reference to the best seen so far.

That's not how I'd expect the patience process/stopping training on plateau to work – I'd expect [early stopping], which is what Scikit-Learn's SGDClassifier does ([docs]). I see early stopping as a proxy for convergence, and a method to

* prevent very bad performance
* limit computation

I do not see it as a method to provide marginal performance gains. At best, holding on to the best model will improve the score by `tol`, at least for real-world use.

I don't think this will be an issue in practice: the variance of validation scores on plateau is typically small (e.g., [CIFAR10 errors] from the [torch blog]). I'm inclined to say this noise is (typically) due to randomness in the dataset or training algorithm.

For real-world models/datasets/problems, adaptive algorithms will succeed in returning the highest scoring model, at least in terms of the final fit score (because they retain the highest scoring model every time they're called). The image I showed earlier isn't realistic, and a low probability result where even a passive search wouldn't return a high performing model.

[docs]:http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/sgd.html#stopping-criterion
[torch blog]:http://torch.ch/blog/2016/02/04/resnets.html
[CIFAR10 errors]:https://raw.githubusercontent.com/torch/torch.github.io/master/blog/_posts/images/resnets_CIFARtesterror_modeldepth.png
[3]:http://papers.nips.cc/paper/6768-qsgd-communication-efficient-sgd-via-gradient-quantization-and-encoding
[early stopping]:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_stopping
[2]:http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/sgd.html#stopping-criterion

It does. The killProcessGroup for Unix calls KillChildren, which sends the signal to the entire group, which includes the root. 
(Signal to -pid goes to the entire group.)

We have to do it manually on Windows because the TerminateProcess syscall (which is what os.Process.Kill uses) doesn't go to the whole group. The implementation of KillChildren for Windows only killed the child processes. So there was a discrepancy in Windows vs Unix behaviors of our KillChildren implementations, which is why killProcessGroup has to also go and kill the root process.

@BaritoneBeard this is my own review; we can wait on @hectormz's as well, but I think the function here doesn't implement `join_conditional`, and its implementation should be changed to implement it correctly.

What's implemented in the function right now is instead a query operation, which is trivially done using `pandas`' existing `.query()` API. For example, one may do:

```python
df.query("left_column < right_column")
```

and we'll be done.

I think what's intended by `join_conditional` is filtering when joining two tables. Looking around online, I see a lot of discussion in the `pandas` dev chatter:

- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/8962
- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/10309

On studying those implementations, I think what's intended is the following implementation:

```python
@pf.register_dataframe_method
def join_conditional(df: pd.DataFrame, other: pd.DataFrame, conditions: List[str], join_kwargs={}):
    """Conditional join between one dataframe and another.

    This is syntactic sugar for joining (not merging) two dataframes and then filtering the results.

    The conditions that are passed in have to be compatible with dataframe query strings.
    For example, if you want to state that `one_column` must be less than `another_column`,
    then your condition string should be `"one_columns < another_column"`.

    Multiple conditions are allowed. 
    """

    joined_df = df.join(other, **join_kwargs)
    for condition in conditions:
        joined_df = joined_df.query(condition)
    return joined_df
```

In general, I think it could be quite helpful to use separate exception hierarchies for (Python) programming errors and errors that arise during the normal EVM execution (runtime errors due to e.g. IO errors would be third category). Since `EthereumError` is a subclass of `ManticoreError`, using it for all exceptions in the Ethereum code base increases the risk that programming errors are inadvertently masked. 

For example, currently the Executor logs and suppresses all exceptions, even asserts, which can make it more difficult to diagnose errors and test fixes. Using separate exception hierarchies could make it easier to let exceptions caused by programming errors bubble up to the parent Manticore process/instance.

So I think it's relevant that if you use Spotless as your integration layer, you only have one interface to worry about, versus "I'll use project.toml to format my python, precommit for my docs, and a shell-script to clang-format for my c-code."

The fact that this project relies on prettier for formatting its docs is a good example that the people who bother to use one autoformatter usually want others, and it's nice to be able to configure them all from one place, which is the whole point of Spotless.

One argument for keeping Spotless as a peer to VCS is that it offers unique features related to slurping info from git history, which can be valuable for projects with strict legal requirements, especially polyglot projects. ```
```
multipart-body := preamble 1*encapsulation 
               close-delimiter epilogue 

encapsulation := delimiter CRLF body-part 

delimiter := CRLF "--" boundary   ; taken from  Content-Type 
field. 
                               ;   when   content-type    is 
multipart 
                             ; There must be no space 
                             ; between "--" and boundary. 

close-delimiter := delimiter "--" ; Again, no  space  before 
"--" 

preamble :=  *text                  ;  to  be  ignored  upon 
receipt. 

epilogue :=  *text                  ;  to  be  ignored  upon 
receipt. 

body-part = <"message" as defined in RFC 822, 
         with all header fields optional, and with the 
         specified delimiter not occurring anywhere in 
         the message body, either on a line by itself 
         or as a substring anywhere.  Note that the 
         semantics of a part differ from the semantics 
         of a message, as described in the text.> 
```
Encapsulation with open boundary is required, otherwise it becomes malformed data.

Yes but with a runtime cost.

* At runtime `Sequence[]` will be slower. Every time the event list is modified, a completely new tuple needs to be created.

I did some investigation on this, in this case we cannot rely on `write` to detect on broken connection, we need to use `read`. We might be able to use broken pipe error as well, but this is probably delayed because usually write would probably return before the data is actually sent to remote host, so most likely we cannot detect on time. `Read` should be able to detect immediately in this case. 

The underlying problem is related to [TCP half open connection](http://www.pcvr.nl/tcpip/tcp_conn.htm), which can happen when remote host crashes thus the TCP connection is not going through normal shutdown process. In this case the connection is "half opened", in that after the remote host crashes, the remote has closed its connection side indicating it's not sending, but we still might be able to send since the direction from us to remote is not closed... in this case if we read, we can detect the scenario immediately.

As for ASIO, this is probably a "feature" instead of a "bug", agreed this is a bit nasty though. Actually I found people have been discussing this [back in 2007.](http://boost.2283326.n4.nabble.com/ASIO-Detecting-broken-connections-td2569211.html)

I think your proposal has some nice properties that I just became aware of so I'm posting them here.

So, initially it may seem like having a `BaseSubConfig` and then derived classes from there doesn't buy us much from subclassing a `BaseTrinityConfig` directly. However the benefits become more obvious when we think about the possible use cases.

E.g Let's say we would have a `BaseTrinityConfig` and then another `Eth1TrinityConfig` for the core Ethereum 1 configs. Now lets say we want to add configs specific to light mode and lets assume they would be reusable across Ethereum 1 and 2. In this model, we would be forced to derive both `Eth1TrinityLightConfig` and `Eth2TrinityLightConfig` and duplicate the light node settings.

In constrast, with the proposed model, it works more like a mixin where we can just reuse the `LightSubConfig` to a compositon of `TrinityConfig`+ `Eth1SubConfig` + `LightSubConfig` as well as `TrinityConfig` + `Eth2SubConfig` + `LightSubConfig`.

So, when I first thought about this, I thought that straight top level inheritance would be just fine here as we are mostly talking about data with barely any behavior. But turns out, this has some nice extra properties (it also has some other nice properties) that just became obvious to me now.

So, thanks for pointing it out @pipermerriam

Generally speaking, it's a bad idea to have a list as a default parameter, due to the following reason:

```
def foobar(L=['a']):
    L.append('b')
    print(L)

foobar()    # ['a', 'b']
foobar()    # ['a', 'b', 'b']
```

While we may not be appending to `path` here, we're introducing a "gotcha" that may come to bite us later on. A better way of doing this is:

```
def foobar(L=None):
    if not L:
        L = []
```

And since we're doing this, we can also do:

```
def foobar(L='.'):
    if not isinstance(L, list):
        L = [L]
```

which provides a nicer API for us to use (rather than always calling the function with a list of length 1).

> If I understood correctly, is there anything that makes us suspect our set of requirements could generate such long builds? 🙂
> > You're right, pip tries to download all the intervals, and when it comes to NumPy and the like that adds a lot of overhead.

Well... when I pinned NumPy's version I discovered the resolver backtracks following dependencies dependencies, which presents two problems:

1. Backtracking is exponential.
2. We don't have a have any control over the way our dependencies manage their own dependencies.

The following are the [solutions proposed](https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/user_guide/#possible-ways-to-reduce-backtracking-occurring) by the Pip folks:

> 1. ALLOW PIP TO COMPLETE ITS BACKTRACKING

Already tried, it doesn't work unless we solve our two major problems (wild dependencies' dependencies and circular dependencies).

> 2. REDUCE THE NUMBER OF VERSIONS PIP WILL TRY TO BACKTRACK THROUGH

Also already tried, cf. supra.

> 3. USE CONSTRAINT FILES OR LOCKFILES

This could be easily done with `pip-tools`, `pipgrip`, `Pipenv`, `Poetry`, etc.

We'd still have to solve the circular dependency problem, though, unless we ignore them —given `pip` allows us to do so, or using `pipgrip` as an alternative.

> 4. BE MORE STRICT ON PACKAGE DEPENDENCIES DURING DEVELOPMENT

Same as 1. and 2.

If you have an edge from types `A -> B`, and you have already enforced the type bound `A` before traversing the edge, then after traversing the edge (per the schema and query structure), you already know you are at a `B` and don't need an explicit `INSTANCEOF` check.

What your query points out, though, is an interesting edge case:
- The edge is defined as from types `A -> B`.
- `B` is a preferred location (due to its filter), so it runs first. The other side of the edge is an eligible location but not preferred.
- However, in the query, the class used on the "out" side of the edge is not `A` but a class `X` that is a subclass of `A`. This `X` is not implied by the schema and query structure, and requires an `INSTANCEOF` check. This check is not applied, producing incorrect results.

I would propose two actions here:
- The presence of a `Filter` that does not contain a `Variable` expression (i.e. filters that use tagged parameters instead of runtime parameters) should not make a location preferred, only eligible. This is because OrientDB (to my knowledge) is not able to take advantage of indexes in situations involving tagged parameters (i.e. `ContextField` expressions). This action does not remove the bug, but does remove an unintended consequence of my rushed implementation here, and I suspect will improve scheduling outcomes for a subset of queries.
- Improve the logic for eliminating `INSTANCEOF` checks on the basis of schema and query structure information, such that the edge case above is eliminated.

We can hide this fact from the user (removing the trailing slash whenever he uses it) or we can only let him use the paths in the format that the API expects.

Additionally, I have created a test to verify and document this behavior
```
    def test_mkdir_new_dir(self):
        ...
        with self.assertRaises(dropbox.exceptions.ApiError):
            self.luigiconn.mkdir(DROPBOX_TEST_TRAILING_SLASH_DIR_TO_CREATE)
            # Path with a trailing slash are considered by Dropbox API as a 'malformed_path'
```

In order not to write wrappers for every tool, we intend to inject a `sentry_sdk.init` call inside a null handler that should be instantiated once and plugged anywhere (it i s a NullHandler!). The following logconf file can be used:

```
[loggers]
keys = root

[handlers]
keys = console, sentry

[formatters]
keys =

[logger_root]
level = INFO
handlers = console, sentry

[handler_console]
class = StreamHandler
args = (sys.stdout,)

[handler_sentry]
class=sentry_sdk.integrations.trytond.TrytondSentryHandler
args = (
    'https://37a9a6ea994a4dfe81ef0f278ab779a1@sentry.io/1824945',
    ('list', 'of', 'silenced', 'logger', 'qualnames'))
```

> I think this is the normal behavior people expect from a Python class. How often do you create a class and start modifying attributes of that class without being explicitly told you can do so? I'd argue that the absence of being told you can modify these values and there only being read only properties defined loosely but reasonably expresses that you shouldn't modify these values.

Right for a class, less for an interface, where mutability is not applying. I would never mess up with a class that is designed to be immutable. However here it is an interface, and the meaning is slightly different: the interface states the existence of getters, but does not say anything about the setters.

It would be perfectly valid to write a mutable class that implements the interface. It would be not for the logic here since Certbot is globally relying on the fact that these properties do not change after the object creation.

No, I do not think there is any sane way in any language to force a class to be immutable when it implements some interface.

However for Python specifically, where we are not talking about real interfaces, official documentation is strongly implying that using `@abc.abstractproperty` alone is associated to a read-only property (https://docs.python.org/3/library/abc.html#abc.abstractproperty). So I am fine with it ^^

I'm about 95% sure that the directive `May be extended, do not override` is a poorly worded instruction that subclasses that define `__init__()` should be sure to call `super().__init__()` and thus "extend" `__init__()`, as opposed to if they didn't call `super()` which would be "overriding".

Evidence for this would be that otherwise "extended" makes no sense, and also that plenty of subclasses of `TCPServer` exist that define their own `__init__()` including the werkzeug dev server we currently use, as well as some examples in stdlib like https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/484edbf9bf1a9e6bae0fcb10a0c165b89ea79295/Lib/logging/config.py#L869-L884

Manually constructing the type on the fly is... new to me :P But if you'd rather not touch `__init__` then it will do.

I think there are two alternatives:

1. Just be ok with lots of kwargs - this feels OK, especially as it has the advantage of the function parameters being explicit.
2. Add a `config` kwarg that accepts a dict of the configurable attributes - this clearly delineates these attributes as 'configurable' but you lose the function parameter introspectability to know what arguments can be passed.

I think because I don't really see the kwargs being passed to `register` and `config` right now as being that different to each other (for example, if a task is cancellable is not really configurable, and more a matter of whether the internal implementation of the task properly handles when a cancel event is raised) my preference is for what I suggested above, which is 1.

It's possible we may have a clearer need for separating configuration from registration in the future, but for now, it seems unnecessary.

This is the wrong object to use. Let's say there is the following model:
```python
class Tag(Model):
    name = CharField(...)

class User(Model):
     random_tags = ManyToManyField(Tag)
```
When the tags of a user change, `instance` here is the instance of `User`, so `instance_verbose_name` is `"user"`, which results in the following log entry:
```json
{"changes": {"user": ["Added", ["1 - tag1", "2 - tag2"]]}}
```
This is clearly wrong, as one would expect to see `"random_tags"` is the key in the changes dict here.

I believe these steps will change now that we have the `mvn deploy` set up to run on PR merge to main branch. I don't think we can use the `mvn release` tasks anymore as currently specified in these docs because the end result would be a merge of the PR to the main branch with a SNAPSHOT version in the pom.xml. I would expect the GitHub Action to then just deploy the new snapshot but not the release that was created in the same PR as the snapshot (because the squashed commit will only contain the final diff of the PR, which is the next snapshot version).

The new workflow will probably look like:
1. Do commit to change to release version
2. Open PR with this release version and merge it
3. Do commit to next snapshot version
4. Open PR with this snapshot version and merge it

Thinking more, I think we might be able to still use `mvn release`, but we wouldn't be able to squash the commits when merging the PR. I believe if each of the commits is merged to the main branch separately, the GitHub Action should still run for each and therefor deploy both the new release and new snapshot. That might be more error prone, though, as it goes against the general PR rules of always preferring squash to merge commits. So maybe it's best to use the manual workflow I listed above...

For now, though, I'd suggest we just replace this whole "Every release" section with a "TODO" to get this PR merged, and then we can tackle the release workflow in a new PR with a new release.

I created a modified version of the sample code from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4827#discussion_r830538558 which would allow setting number of threads for `params` in an environment variable (i.e. not relying on `OMP_NUM_THREADS`), and put that in a script.

<details><summary>test-pr-4827.py (click me)</summary>

```python
import os
import json
import numpy as np
import lightgbm as lgb

from sklearn.datasets import make_regression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

ret = []
for i in range(1, 1001):
    if i % 50 == 0:
        print(f"attempt {i}")
    X, y = make_regression(n_samples=10_000, n_features=10, n_informative=5, random_state=42)
    X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)
    lgb_train = lgb.Dataset(X_train, y_train)
    lgb_eval = lgb.Dataset(X_test, y_test, reference=lgb_train)
    params1 = {
        'boosting': 'goss',
        'metric': 'l2',
        'verbose': -1,
        'num_thread': int(os.getenv("LGB_THREADS", "-1"))
    }
    evals_result1 = {}
    gbm = lgb.train(params1, lgb_train,
                    num_boost_round=10,
                    valid_sets=lgb_eval,
                    callbacks=[lgb.record_evaluation(evals_result1)])
    params2 = {
        'boosting': 'goss',
        'metric': 'l2',
        'verbose': -1,
        'num_thread': int(os.getenv("LGB_THREADS", "-1"))
    }
    evals_result2 = {}
    gbm = lgb.train(params2, lgb_train,
                    num_boost_round=10,
                    valid_sets=lgb_eval,
                    callbacks=[lgb.record_evaluation(evals_result2)])
    ret.append(evals_result1['valid_0']['l2'])
    ret.append(evals_result2['valid_0']['l2'])

with open("out.json", "w") as f:
    f.write(json.dumps(ret, indent=4))

ret = np.array(ret)

assert (ret == ret[0]).all()
```

</details>

(1) client creates an AuthorizedSession

When I built the docs locally, I saw that `__init__()` docs for `DaskLGBMClassifier`, `DaskLGBMRegressor`, and `DaskLGBMRanker` have the `client` doc added, and the docs for their scikit-learn equivalents do not. **DaskLGBMClassifier.__init__()** ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7608904/106378251-d79be980-6368-11eb-88d5-af50c2d56c8c.png) **LGBMClassifier.__init__()** ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7608904/106378244-ce128180-6368-11eb-8fc3-f509d9f407b3.png) ### Why I think copying is the best alternative I tried several other ways to update the docstrings, but none of them quite worked. I'll explain in terms of `*Classifier`, but this applies for `*Regressor` and `*Ranker` as well. 1. Editing `DaskLGBMClassifier.__init__.doc` ```python class DaskLGBMClassifier(LGBMClassifier, _DaskLGBMModel): ... _base_doc = LGBMClassifier.__init__.__doc__ _before_kwargs, _kwargs, _after_kwargs = _base_doc.partition('**kwargs') DaskLGBMClassifier.__init__.__doc__ = ( _before_kwargs + 'client : dask.distributed.Client or None, optional (default=None)\n' + ' ' * 12 + 'Dask client. If ``None``, ``distributed.default_client()`` will be used at runtime. This client will not be saved if the model object is pickled.\n' + ' ' * 8 + _kwargs + _after_kwargs ) ``` Doing this for each of the 3 model objects, the API docs show 3 copies of the doc for `client`. Those 3 copies also show up on the docs for `lightgbm.sklearn.LGBMClassifier`. 2. Setting up a pass-through `__init__()` ```python class DaskLGBMClassifier(LGBMClassifier, _DaskLGBMModel): def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super().__init__(*args, **kwargs) _base_doc = __init__.__doc__ _before_kwargs, _kwargs, _after_kwargs = _base_doc.partition('**kwargs') __init__.__doc__ = ( _before_kwargs + 'client : dask.distributed.Client or None, optional (default=None)\n' + ' ' * 12 + 'Dask client. If ``None``, ``distributed.default_client()`` will be used at runtime. This client will not be saved if the model object is pickled.\n' + ' ' * 8 + _kwargs + _after_kwargs ) ``` This results in this error at runtime. ```text doing this to avoid: RuntimeError: scikit-learn estimators should always specify their parameters in the signature of their __init__ (no varargs). with constructor (self, *args, **kwargs) doesn't follow this convention. ``` 3. Just copying `__init__` Both of these variations: ```python class DaskLGBMClassifier(LGBMClassifier, _DaskLGBMModel): __init__ = LGBMClassifier.__init__ _base_doc = __init__.__doc__ _before_kwargs, _kwargs, _after_kwargs = _base_doc.partition('**kwargs') __init__.__doc__ = ( _before_kwargs + 'client : dask.distributed.Client or None, optional (default=None)\n' + ' ' * 12 + 'Dask client. If ``None``, ``distributed.default_client()`` will be used at runtime. This client will not be saved if the model object is pickled.\n' + ' ' * 8 + _kwargs + _after_kwargs ) ``` Doing this for each of the 3 model objects, the API docs show 3 copies of the doc for `client`. Those 3 copies also show up on the docs for `lightgbm.sklearn.LGBMClassifier`. (same as in the image above)

The idea of clearing [`storage_formats/__init__.py`](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/cf1e5416a1ba4b072d3719bcfa94b8dbaddfdbb4/modin/core/storage_formats/__init__.py) and then reverting it back when `PyDbEngine` and `pyarrow.gandiva` would become compatible feels wrong to me. There we fixed the problem of unnecessary `pyarrow` import by [wrapping it into `try-except` construction](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/cf1e5416a1ba4b072d3719bcfa94b8dbaddfdbb4/modin/core/storage_formats/__init__.py#L20-L25) with a note, that we can do a [lazy submodules import after we bump the minimum python version to 3.7+](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/pull/2126#discussion_r493394176), which we actually did about a year ago (#2615). So I don't see a reason why we should make a test checking that `PyDBEngine` and `pyarrow.gandiva` are incompatible since it's not Modin's care. The only thing that is logical to me is to test that the natural Modin initialization flow works properly witn OmniSci.

Currently, there are two circumstances that lead to `OnWorkerIdle` being called on a thief while it is stealing:

1. Because the stolen tasks are forwarded to the thief individually (by the victim's `PushNormalTask` callbacks), a stolen task can complete its execution on the thief worker node (and thus respond to the owner by activating the thief's `PushNormalTask` callback, which in turn calls `OnWorkerIdle` on the thief) before all stolen tasks have been forwarded to the thief.
2. If new tasks are added to the task queue while stealing is in progress, `SubmitTask` will call `OnWorkerIdle` to submit the new tasks to a worker, and this worker can be the thief itself. In fact, I've seen this happen before. This scenario (tasks being added to the queue after work stealing has begun) is possible because sometimes there are some lags in the process of filling up the task queue. When one such lag is long enough, the queue can become empty and a worker can finish its load (thus entering the work stealing mode) before more tasks are added to the queue.

My opinion on the two solutions: 

> 1# Add `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress` as a new subclass of `NetifMulticastAddress` with `MlrState` being it's member. 
> 2# Add an of MlrState with equal size of  mExtMulticastAddressPool.

First of all, I think they are similar solutions without much practical difference. 
- 1# is more object oriented by encapsulating MlrState in the `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress` class, but it requires significant code refactoring. For example,  `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress` will be put to a `LinkedList<ExternalNetifMulticastAddress>` but `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress.GetNext()` returns a `NetifMulticastAddress`, which violates the protocol of `LinkedList`. 
- 2# introduces much less change to existing code but it assumes that a unique index can be get for each external multicast address (e.g. allocated from a array of a Pool). 2# also allows using a bit-vector with 2 bits for each address to further reduce the memory consumption, even though the benefit is minor (e.g. 8 multicast addresses consumes 8B in solution 1# but only 2B in solution 2#). 

Overall I prefer 2# for a little bit because it involves less code change.

How about instead of passing `aAddressIndex` to the `Get/SetMlrState` methods, we require the caller to pass a reference to the `aAddress` entry itself.
-  We require `aAddress` reference to be the same reference as the one user does get from iterating over `Child` addresses (i.e. same reference that we get when we loop over addresses using `IterateIp6Addresses()`).
- Then inside `Child` class, we simply check the address of `aAddress` against `mIp6Address[]` to find an index which we can use to get to two masks `mMlrToRegisterMask` and `mMlrRegisteredMask`

I feel this model may be more straight-forward than current model of using an `AddressIterator` to go through addresses, converting the iterator to an index, then passing the index to `Child` methods get/set more info/state about the address of the child.

When using the `az sig image-version update` command, if the customer does not pass in the `--target-edge-zones` parameter, the existing target edge zones will be cleared. This behavior may be a bug for users, the main reasons are as follows:
1. When users use the `az sig image-version update` command to update other properties, they may not consider that the target edge zones will be affected. It is an unexpected behavior for them to clear target edge zones.
2. This approach is inconsistent with the common design of other CLI commands. We usually recommend that the user specify a special value to indicate emptying the existing propertites. If the user does not pass in the relevant parameters, this property will not be updated. Such as the `--nsg-rule NONE` for `az vm create` indicates that users want to clear the nsg rule.

So this design which looks different from other commands looks more like a bug. So I suggest keeping the design consistent with other commands to let the user explicitly enter `--target-edge-zones NONE` to indicate clearing the existing target edge zones.

```python
from functools import wraps

from intervals import interval
from sqlalchemy.orm.base import NO_VALUE


def overflow_patch(func):
    "https://github.com/kvesteri/intervals/issues/42"

    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(self, arg):
        if arg == NO_VALUE:
            return func(self, None)
        return func(self, arg)

    return wrapper


def apply_patches():

    interval.AbstractInterval.__eq__ = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.__eq__)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__gt__ = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.__gt__)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__lt__ = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.__lt__)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__ge__ = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.__ge__)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__le__ = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.__le__)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__contains__ = overflow_patch(
        interval.AbstractInterval.__contains__
    )
    interval.AbstractInterval.__add__ = overflow_patch(
        interval.AbstractInterval.__add__
    )
    interval.AbstractInterval.__sub__ = overflow_patch(
        interval.AbstractInterval.__sub__
    )
    interval.AbstractInterval.glb = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.glb)
    interval.AbstractInterval.lub = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.lub)
    interval.AbstractInterval.inf = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.inf)
    interval.AbstractInterval.sup = overflow_patch(interval.AbstractInterval.sup)
    interval.AbstractInterval.__rsub__ = overflow_patch(
        interval.AbstractInterval.__rsub__
    )

```

> I feel like these 2 classes are rather awkward, but I'll merge it for now.
> 

Yeah, it's a bit awkward because the tests aren't being performed **_directly_** on `BaseCache`, and because of how many abstract methods need to be defined on the derived class.  However the definitions of `AbstractBaseCache` and `ConcreteBaseCache` are trivial and transparent, so at least no new complexity is added.


> I don't think there's any value in testing the logic of abstract methods, and I'm wondering if the other tests would still be testing the same thing if we just use the memory cache instead.. If so, we could then drop both of these classes.

On the contrary, I really like how the logic of what's defined in `BaseCache` gets tested independently with the (abstract) base class.  

The derived classes used for the backend implementations use more resources and add enough "side effects" that proper testing of the underlying logic would require repeating these tests with all 3 backends.  And even then, it would perhaps be an open question of whether the `BaseCache` functionality extends to new backends.

Keeping these tests of `BaseCache` separate from tests of the backends seems much cleaner and more robust. Previously this was a bit simpler because `BaseCache` was not formally abstract--it had unimplemented methods intended to be override, but instantiating `BaseCache` did not raise an exception.

Well, in any case, I'm open to finding a cleaner test solution, but I favor keeping the `BaseCache` tests separate from the backend tests of the same methods.

the DagsterLogManager has two purposes -- the first is to log messages from internal sources (i.e. DagsterEvents), and the other is to allow users to add their own messages to the event log (i.e. context.log.info("my custom message") in user-provided solid code). With this diff, the DagsterLogManager inherits from the logging.Logger class, so all of its .info(), .debug(), .error() etc. functions still exist, but don't need to be specified here. In fact, you could still log a dagster event without the log_dagster_event function (it would look like log_manager.info(msg, extra={"dagster_meta": dagster_event)). However, logging dagster events this way looked a little brittle to me (if you don't know what you're doing, it's pretty easy to mess up that "extra" field, as it needs to be formatted that specific way), so for convenience I added a log_dagster_event function, with explicit parameters, to make that specific use case cleaner.

It looks like this appears in destroy, up, preview and refresh. I suspect we will always want these variables to be the same across commands. 
To avoid leaking how remote works (and risking inconsistencies across commands), we can encapsulate these in a struct:
```go
// Flags for remote operations.
type RemoteArgs struct {
  remote bool
  envVars []string
  preRunCommands []string
  gitBranch string
  ...
}

// Add flags to support remote operations
func (r *RemoteArgs) applyFlags(cmd *cobra.Command) {
    cmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVar(
		&r.remote, "remote", false,
		"[EXPERIMENTAL] Run the operation remotely")
  cmd.PersistentFlags().StringArrayVar(
		&r.envVars, "remote-env", []string{},
		"[EXPERIMENTAL] Environment variables to use in the remote operation of the form NAME=value for "+
			"plaintext values or NAME#=value for secret values (e.g. `--remote-env FOO=bar "+
			"--remote-env BAR#=secret`)")
  ...
}
```

and then initialize them like so:

```go
// With the other variables
remoteArgs := RemoteArgs{}
...

// With the other flags
remoteArgs.applyFlags(cmd)
```

@chrisdicaprio Working with this GMM I found the small issue here, which is that in this case that the total standard deviation contains a dependency on the nonlinear amplification term then `tau` should be returned as `(1.0 + NL) ** 2 * tau ** 2` (see the original Chiou & Youngs implementation here: https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/blob/master/openquake/hazardlib/gsim/chiou_youngs_2014.py#L370). This doesn't affect the classical PSHA calculations as they rely only on `sigma`, but it does affect the event-based risk calculations where between- and within-event residuals are sampled separately, so the total variability of the GMM would be different in the event-based case compared to the classical PSHA case.

Yes, `httpx` can take `bytes`. That's why the `content` is encoded back to `bytes` in `_from_serialized_response` when constructing `httpx.Response`. The decode is for serializing the VCR response, not for `httpx`. If you take out the decode, I believe you will get `!!binary` in the serialized yaml even for plain text. I guess reason of decoding the data into unicode is trying to have human readable yaml file. This unicode / bytes conversion happens in other libraries as well, but it is done in for the [body](https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy/blob/c79a06f639dd628536c9868044e78df1012985f9/vcr/serializers/compat.py#L54) field only. Therefore, I suggest the better fix should be having a consistent serialization schema for all libraries, which is discussed in #463.

If there are many calls to this this will certainly be expensive.  We may want to avoid calling this procedure at all and be a bit more conservative about the requirements of what is in `args.path`.  For example, we may require that this list contains either valid folder paths--as verified by a single call made at the beginning to `project-xxxx/describe` or a string of the form `project-xxxx:file-yyyy` which can be validated by a single bulk call, e.g. to `findDataObjects` or something like that.  This approach would minimize API calls made to the platform by performing bulk operations when necessary and minimize load to the platform.  Another approach would be to separate out the arguments into two groups: folders and files.  Files must always be accompanied with a 'project' to help us minimize load.  For this feature in particular I think it's important to think about the scale of the inputs because my feeling is that users tend to like to archive lots of files at a time.  It's natural to do that by folder or project, but I can see users providing lots of files in some cases and IMHO and we should either (1) address this in the design as a limit we impose and error out on the client side or (2) protect ourselves via the method described above.

The `_load` method on both `BaseRequest` and `BaseResponse` return instances of the class from the data source.  The question is where that data comes from, and there are four possibilities each for both `RequestBase` and `ResponseBase`:

  * `unit.received` for data coming from a single unit on the remote side of the relation
  * `relation.received_app` for data coming from the leader of the remote side of the unit to represent the entire app (new in this PR)
  * `unit.to_publish` for data that this unit published for itself only
  * `relation.to_publish_app` for data that the leader of this app published for this entire app (new in this PR, and can only be read or written by the leader)

The original set of classes was written assuming that the data would only ever come in over unit-level data (since app-level data wasn't available then). It also was (poorly, by me) designed to try to track whether the data was being read or published (hence the `is_received` and `_source_data` attributes), so needs to handle both cases.  (Doing it again, the classes would just be de/serializers for a given data bag, and the relation class would be responsible for keeping track of which data bag a given request or response needs to be associated with, since it's much easier to do cleanly at that level.)

All that said, the issue is that you've added a way to read request data from the app-level relation data, but you haven't added a way to write request data to the app-level relation data nor have you added a way to either read or write response data from/to app-level relation data.

Here's the error case we need to be careful about: if `__init__` raises an exception, `__del__` is still called. If `__init__` raises an exception before it initializes `self._handle`, then when `__del__` tries to access `self._handle`, it will raise `AttributeError` and the [Python interpreter will print a warning](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__del__).

There are a lot of different ways to solve this:
1. Change `__init__` to set `self._handle = None` before calling anything that can raise an exception. This is the path you are on.
2. Change `__init__` to use try/except to initialize `self._handle = None` in error cases.
3. Change `__del__` to use `getattr(self, "_handle", None)`.
4. Change `__del__` to use try/except.

We don't need to initialize `self._close_on_exit` twice. 
- `__del__` doesn't use `self._close_on_exit`.
- `__exit__` uses `self._close_on_exit`, but `__exit__` is only called after `__init__` succeeds. `__exit__` may be called after `__enter__` raises an exception, according to https://docs.python.org/3/reference/compound_stmts.html#the-with-statement

To continue with solution 1, please make the following changes to both `Task` and `WatchdogTask`:
- Delete `self._close_on_exit = False`.
- Put this at the top of `__init__`, right below the docstring. Change `new_task_name` to `task_name` for `WatchdogTask`.
```
# Initialize the fields that __del__ accesses so it doesn't crash when __init__ raises an exception.
self._handle = None
self._saved_task = new_task_name
```

We can't easily set a timeout for a single request without affecting other users of the raylet client pool, so I propose a different rule instead:

* Each raylet can have one inflight heartbeat broadcast.
* If a raylet has an inflight request, drop future broadcasts until the request finishes. 

This is no worse than Redis's current guarantees still, and in the happy case (all requests finish on time), it's guaranteed to work correctly (this is still better than piggybacking, because even in the happy case, max concurrency would make it difficult to track the batches).
